county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1875 as Shannon County; renamed Oglala Lakota County in 2015. Entirely within Pine Ridge Indian Reservation. Site of the Wounded Knee Massacre (1890). The county is unorganized and has no county government separate from tribal governance.
county_metadata.research_tips: Oglala Lakota County (formerly Shannon County until 2015) is entirely within Pine Ridge Indian Reservation and has no separate county government. BIA Pine Ridge Agency records at NARA Kansas City are the primary genealogical source — Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records under the Dawes Act, school records (Pine Ridge Boarding School, Holy Rosary Mission/Red Cloud Indian School), and agency correspondence document Oglala Lakota families. The Wounded Knee Massacre (1890) records are at NARA — military records, investigation reports, and survivor accounts. The Oglala Lakota College library has tribal historical materials. Catholic mission records (Holy Rosary Mission, 1888) are at Marquette University's Bureau of Catholic Indian Missions collection. Episcopal church records (missions on Pine Ridge) document converts and families. Tribal enrollment records at Pine Ridge Agency. The SD State Historical Society holds related territorial and state records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1875 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1875. Register of Deeds issued licenses; state registration began 1905.

source_profiles.source_name: Oglala Lakota County Clerk of Courts Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate, guardianship, adoption, naturalization, civil and criminal case files from 1875. Clerk of Courts maintains court records for the circuit court.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Oglala Lakota County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Oglala Lakota County including homestead, preemption, timber culture, and cash entries. South Dakota was entirely public domain — GLO records are the primary source for first land ownership.
Federal population schedules for Oglala Lakota County. Dakota Territory enumerated from 1860. Agricultural schedules (1850-1880) provide additional family and economic data.

source_profiles.repository: South Dakota State Historical Society / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers from Oglala Lakota County communities. SDSHS holds the largest collection of SD newspapers on microfilm. Chronicling America has digitized SD papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1875 during the Black Hills gold rush. Rapid City is SD's second-largest city. Named for John L. Pennington, Dakota Territory governor. Home to Mount Rushmore.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pennington County Register of Deeds in Rapid City holds deed and mining records from 1875. Rapid City is SD's second-largest city and the gateway to the Black Hills and Mount Rushmore. The Journey Museum in Rapid City has local history collections. BLM GLO homestead and mineral patent records are essential. Pennington County Clerk of Courts has extensive probate, naturalization, and civil records. Ellsworth Air Force Base (1942+) records at NARA document military personnel. The Rapid City Journal newspaper (1878+) is SD's second-most important newspaper, on microfilm at SDSHS. Black Hills mining camp records document early gold rush settlers. Mount Rushmore construction records (1927-1941) at NARA identify workers. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county spans from the Black Hills to the Badlands, creating diverse settlement patterns.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1908 from Harding and Butte counties. Named for Henry Perkins, state senator. Ranching county in northwest SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Perkins County Register of Deeds in Bison holds deed records from 1908. BLM GLO homestead records document settlers who filed claims during the 1909-1918 homesteading boom. Perkins County Clerk of Courts in Bison has probate and civil records. Cattle and sheep ranching are the primary economy; livestock brand records at the SD Brand Board identify ranching families. German-Russian and Scandinavian settlers arrived with the homesteading wave. The Bison Courier newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. Harding and Butte counties hold pre-1908 records. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed county records. Many homestead claims in Perkins County were abandoned during drought years — BLM final certificate records document both successful and failed claims.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1875. Named for Joel Potter, Dakota Territory legislator. Gettysburg (county seat) was named after the Civil War battle.
county_metadata.research_tips: Potter County Register of Deeds in Gettysburg holds deed records from 1875. Oahe Dam and Lake Oahe (1948-1962) flooded Missouri River bottomlands in western Potter County — pre-dam homestead and land records at BLM are important for displaced families. BLM GLO homestead records document original settlers. Potter County Clerk of Courts in Gettysburg has probate, naturalization, and civil records. German-Russian settlers were prominent — check GRHS in Bismarck, ND and German Evangelical church records. The Gettysburg Potter County News newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Forest City, an early town flooded by Lake Oahe, had its own newspaper and records. Cattle ranching and farming are the primary economies.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1883 from Grant County. Named for S.G. Roberts, Dakota Territory legislator. Contains the Sisseton-Wahpeton Oyate (Lake Traverse Reservation).
county_metadata.research_tips: Roberts County Register of Deeds in Sisseton holds deed records from 1883. The Sisseton-Wahpeton Oyate (Lake Traverse Reservation) covers much of Roberts County — BIA records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, and school records for Sisseton and Wahpeton Dakota families. Fort Sisseton (1864-1889) records at NARA document the military post established after the 1862 Dakota War. BLM GLO homestead records document settlers on opened reservation lands. Roberts County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Sisseton Courier newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. The Sisseton-Wahpeton tribal enrollment office has tribal membership records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Grant County holds some pre-1883 records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1883 from Miner County. Named for George W. Sanborn, railroad official. Agricultural county in east-central SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sanborn County Register of Deeds in Woonsocket holds deed records from 1883. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. Norwegian, Swedish, and German settlers predominated. Sanborn County Clerk of Courts in Woonsocket has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The Woonsocket News newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Lutheran church records are essential for the Scandinavian community. The county was settled during the Great Dakota Boom (1878-1887) and experienced population decline during drought years. Miner County holds pre-1883 records. Railroad land sale records at the Newberry Library identify early settlers recruited by colonization agents.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873 from Hanson County. Named for S.L. Spink, Dakota Territory secretary. Agricultural county; Redfield was a railroad town.
county_metadata.research_tips: Spink County Register of Deeds in Redfield holds deed records from 1873. Redfield was a Chicago and North Western Railway town — railroad colonization and land sale records identify settlers. BLM GLO homestead records document original claims. Spink County Clerk of Courts in Redfield has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Norwegian, German, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. The Redfield Press newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Spink County Historical Society has local family files. The SD State Developmental Center in Redfield (1902+) has institutional records at SDSHS. Lutheran and Catholic church records are essential. The county was heavily settled during the Great Dakota Boom.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873. Named for David S. Stanley, US Army general. Fort Pierre is one of SD's oldest Euro-American settlements, dating to the fur trade era.
county_metadata.research_tips: Stanley County Register of Deeds in Fort Pierre holds deed records from 1873. Fort Pierre was one of the earliest Euro-American settlements in SD — the American Fur Company established Fort Pierre Chouteau (1832) as a major Missouri River trading post. Fur trade records at the Missouri Historical Society (St. Louis) and SDSHS document the earliest families, including French-Indian mixed-blood (Metis) families. BLM GLO homestead records cover later settlement. Stanley County Clerk of Courts has probate and civil records. The Fort Pierre Times newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. Oahe Dam (1948-1962) dramatically changed the local landscape; pre-dam records at BLM are important. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Fort Pierre was a parent county for several western SD counties; check SDSHS for early territorial records. Cattle ranching dominated after the fur trade era.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873. Named for Alfred Sully, US Army general (led punitive expeditions against Sioux, 1863-1865). Agricultural and ranching county in central SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sully County Register of Deeds in Onida holds deed records from 1873. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. General Sully's 1863 expedition against the Sioux passed through this area — military records at NARA document the campaign. Sully County Clerk of Courts in Onida has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Scandinavian and German settlers predominated. The Onida Watchman newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Oahe Dam (1948-1962) impacted western portions of the county; pre-dam records at BLM document affected homesteads. Lutheran and Catholic church records serve the primary ethnic communities. The county's ranching heritage is documented in livestock brand records at the SD Brand Board.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1909. Entirely within Rosebud Indian Reservation. The county is unorganized and has no county government separate from Rosebud Sioux tribal governance. Named for John B.S. Todd, Dakota Territory delegate.
county_metadata.research_tips: Todd County is entirely within Rosebud Indian Reservation and has no separate county government. BIA Rosebud Agency records at NARA Kansas City are the primary genealogical source — Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, school records (St. Francis Mission School, Rosebud Boarding School), and agency correspondence document Sicangu Lakota families. Catholic mission records (St. Francis Mission, 1886) are at Marquette University's Bureau of Catholic Indian Missions collection. Episcopal mission records on the reservation document converts and families. Tribal enrollment records at the Rosebud Agency office. BLM GLO records cover land allotments. The Todd County Tribune and Rosebud newspapers at SDSHS cover community news. The Sinte Gleska University has tribal historical materials. SDSHS holds related state records.
